speaker: interviewer
text: In the interview with Mr. Stefan Stok. So at the end of the last tape, you told me two things. First, you were left in this staircase hiding at the curfew time, after you were almost caught in this tram. And then you told me a little bit about the burning of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The uprising in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The uprising, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The uprising, that's right. The uprising was, of course, something that touched me very personally, because my father was still there. My father was still in the ghetto, and some other family members. And my father was in the Schultz shop, because Schultz ship, I think, was not itself completely destroyed yet during the uprising. So what happened there to workers in the shop of Schultz, and to the workers in the shop of Toebbens, that's a digression, was that they were promised by the Germans that, although the ghetto is now destroyed and won't exist anymore, the workers are going to continue to work for the Wehrmacht, for the German army, except that they are going to be moved out of the ghetto and taken into a labor camp in Lublin area of Poland. And that was not like this time the Germans really took some people, management and so on, to show them that they mean business, that they really take those Jews to the labor camps, and not to any kind of destruction. And it was all-- all done with many promises from the German management, and as well I think from the SS, probably that those workers have nothing to fear. As long as they work for the German army, they will survive the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father was in one of the last groups who remained in this factory. And lots of those workers, and machines as well, that moved out of the ghetto into Trawniki, which was this labor camp in the Lublin area. There were two such camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Poniatowa, which was done for the workers of Toebbens, of another huge factory in ghetto. And Trawniki was for Schultz. And there were many people who moved into those camps. I don't know exactly how many. But in Trawniki, must have been something like, I don't know, thousands of them, maybe 10,000. And my father was then evacuated, taken out of ghetto on a lorry in the beginning of May, already in the middle of the uprising. And they were taken to the lorry to Trawniki. And the lorry stopped to tank more petrol in a garage in the west of Warsaw. And my father then-- and they were waiting for petrol, and waiting for some German escort. And my father jumped out of that lorry then. And he managed to find a connection in Warsaw, and went into hiding. As for the remainder of those who went to Trawniki, they really went to Trawniki. And they really went to Poniatowa. And they were kept there for-- and working there-- but in Trawniki and Poniatowa were both relatively acceptable camps. People were not-- they were labor camps. They were, of course, not exactly an easygoing place. But people were, I think, treated relatively humanely. And it was even possible to get out of them without too many problems. You-- one could escape from them. They were not guarded all that closely until beginning of November, 1943, when the Germans surrounded, quite without any warning, those camps, and shot everybody dead. That was, I think, on the side among them an uncle of mine, and so on, who was still there by this time. So that happened on the third of November, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, coming back-- Do yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming back, the ghetto was burning. And I was in this house at the end stop of the tram, and spent the night there. And as soon as it was light in the morning, as soon as the curfew was over-- I think at 5:00 o'clock in the morning or something like that-- I went down. And I manage-- I think I caught a-- again, jumped on a tram, and came to the place where-- where we lived in [NON-ENGLISH], came to the house. And in the gate, in this typical Polish entrance, a Warsaw entrance hole, I met my mother, who was running out of there, out of her mind with red eyes, crying, terribly upset, because she was, of course, sure that since I didn't appear there for the night that I was now already gone, and dead, and finished. And probably, she heard some shooting during the night. And she thought that it was the Gestapo who caught me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they all-- they were certain that that was the end of me. And she-- now, suddenly, I appeared. So she was overjoyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the other hand, Miss [NON-ENGLISH], who kept us, thought that really was-- she was afraid once too more-- once too much-- or-- or too strongly. And she told us that, unfortunately, we have to leave. So we had no choice. We left there. And we had to look for another place. And we-- I don't know how my mother exactly looked for those places, who exactly gave her this place. But we left. And we went to that place into some room, or flat, of some of the house-- how to say the porter of the house in the old town of Warsaw in Pivna. But once we came there in the afternoon, we somehow had the feeling that people were after us, that somebody is blackmailing us. And, indeed, somebody came and demanded money. And we had to leave that place in Pivna only after a very few-- after a few hours. And we-- when it was slightly darker, not even dark, because it was in May. The days were long. We left there through the somehow back exit, and went somewhere else, and found some other place to stay for a day or two. And then we found another place to stay for a couple of days. And then we found a place in the old town market, which is the center of the old city, an old historic house, which is now-- it's a very fancy and touristy place. But it wasn't then. And we had some room there, where I had to spend most of the time on the floor, because if you got up, you could be seen from the-- from the street. And so we were hiding from-- we were hiding, changing flats every two days, or every three days, and finding still-- managing somehow to survive, in spite of being all the time set by the blackmailers, set on. The blackmailers, as I said, were those who were all the time trying to get money out of you. And there were plenty of them in this area as well. And there was somebody who helped us to find those places. But I didn't realize it in the beginning. But then I met-- this chap came once or twice to help us to move into another place. And his name was Koper, Stefan Koper. And he was a middle-aged chap, a Pole of-- must have been then maybe around 40 or thereabouts. And with-- and he, somehow for not very obvious reason to me, he helped us again and again to find a place. And then we moved out-- all the time, my mother and I, we moved out of this area, old town of Warsaw, and found a place in the east part of Warsaw, on the other side of the Vistula in Praga, which is a quite a large part of Warsaw on the east side of the Vistula. And we lived there for a week or so in one place, in one street. And then there was a horrendous bombardment when we were there. But somehow, I was not afraid of the bombardment. We're standing down at the entrance to the-- on the ground floor in the courtyard. And we saw the-- the lights of the reflectors in the sky seeking for the planes, and the bombs, and the-- the anti-aircraft fire, which was some of it was visible, and the bombs. It all came that were the Russians, Russian bombardment. And some of it came very close. We heard bombs very close. But we were so happy to see them coming, all the same. We were not-- the bombardments were something that was not bothering me. And later, I was actually sleeping through the bombardments, not even-- at Kopers, there was not, where we were later, there was no shelter anyway. There was a cellar, which was-- could maybe save you from a bullet if you-- but not from a bomb. And so there was a-- but the bombardment somehow was not something to be afraid of. And then we, again, changed place, and lived with some Ukrainian woman for a few days. And my-- and my mother went somewhere. And this Ukrainian woman took me by the hand, and took me somewhere else. I think she decided that I was a risk factor in some way. And she has to take me somewhere else. And she took me to the flat of Koper, who brought me there, because he found us this flat on Praga. He found us probably a number of flats in the last few weeks. So she took us to Koper's flat. And that was somewhere in Praga. And Koper appeared and saw me. And he was not-- didn't make a-- he knew me, because we met already a couple of times. He came there a couple of times. And he saw me. He was not happy. He was not at all happy. He was not at all welcoming. On the other hand, he didn't throw me out. So-- and the Ukrainian woman left me, and went away. And she said to Copper-- I don't know what she said. She said, basically, I can't keep this boy any longer. You keep him for a while here, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was all without my mother even knew that it happened. And then Koper came there. And-- and who was Koper? And what was his function?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't understand. So they brought-- they left me for a while, and then brought me to another room in the flat. It was only a small flat, and left me again. And, suddenly, my father appeared. That was an amazing thing, because after all I didn't see my father now for over three months. I last time saw him in the ghetto. And although I knew that he was-- got out of the ghetto, I didn't know-- I was not told where he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is the time where he escaped from this lorry, it was about this time? That's well after. It was about two weeks later. And what happened was that Koper started hiding Jews. And the first person that he hid, or one of the very first, was my Aunt Hania, who came to him more or less straight of the ghetto, and who is still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after her, somebody else came into this flat, and somebody else, and somebody else, and my father, after he escaped from that Lorry was somehow brought there as well. And then I was brought there. And nobody was thrown out. And, suddenly, there were 11 Jews hiding in this little flat, apart from the house owners, who were a couple, Koper and his wife Marta, Marta. And a lodger-- there was a lodger as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Pole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: A Pole, a Polish lodger, and 11 or 12 Jewish lodgers who were, of course, not official. So that all happened. And that was all in the-- in this flat, in this single story building, wooden building, in the-- in Praga, quite in the center of Praga, quit in the-- not far even from the Vistula, in the area which is pretty central. But because Praga was always kind of a neglected part of the city, that was a large terrain which was left-- which was never developed fully as an urban area. Only there were some blocks of flats there built by the army, I think, for the families again before the war, so some pretty new blocks of flats, and some allotment gardens, and some houses, wooden houses, almost barracks of a kind, where people were-- families were living.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it didn't look like in the middle of the city. It looked like an outskirts, like some kind of a pretty vague area on the outskirts of the city. Anyway, it was in this pretty much in the center. And this house, this wooden house where Koper lived, Koper got it because it was this flat-- because he worked for the army, or for the border guard director, something like that, before the war. And that belonged-- all the flats, belonged, I think, to the army, or to army formations. And they lived there almost as if it was in the country. It was-- the front door was leading to the garden. They had a garden in front, a large garden, where Marta, the wife of Koper, was having a beautiful garden with vegetables, and so on. And because of the garden, you couldn't exactly see who was coming in front. And even the front window was not very-- was covered by vegetation. And side windows were open to the street. It was not-- the street was really some distance away. You had to walk about 100 meters to come to the street. And it was a bit like in the country in a way, although it was, as I said, pretty much in the center of Warsaw. And the wife of Koper, Marta, she was on the whole a peasant. She was a peasant woman. She looked like a peasant woman, with big red cheeks, and quite a sturdy woman. She must have there in her forties, no children, and very strong, and strongly built, and working in the garden. That was her favorite place. She had, as well, another place in the country near Warsaw, about 10 kilometers-- no 20 kilometers-- from Warsaw, where they had a tiny little house, and allotment. And they were going there to plant-- to look after the orchard, and the garden, and so on. And she was really-- she came from the country. She was a peasant. And, suddenly, here she was-- her husband was a petty official. And, suddenly, God knows why exactly, because they never were particularly, one would have thought inclined or interested, they suddenly got into this business of keeping Jews, saving Jews. In the beginning, it must have been purely Koper to earn some money, in the beginning. And, somehow, he got involved in that. And somehow, suddenly, those Jews were human beings. And they didn't feel like throwing anybody out. And though in the beginning people paid them to be there-- I mean Hania probably paid them and so on. Later, they finished up with quite a lot of people who didn't have any money. And they kept them. They even bought food for them. There was-- I mean, the poorer Jews were financed by the richer Jews in that case. But Kopers didn't have much out of it. And they had a lot of trouble, because they had, after all, to keep them somehow nourished. They had-- Marta had to go, and to buy food. And every time there was-- and there were other people living in this-- just behind the wall. And we were-- and any louder word could-- could finish with all of us. If neighbors were suspicious, it would be enough, a word from a neighbor, to bury all of us on the spot, practically. But we were lucky. The neighbors never-- they were never found out. The neighbors made jokes about Marta carrying big baskets of food. But they never, somehow, found out. They made jokes about the fact that the windows were always covered with curtains. But, somehow, they didn't suspect completely. And those Jews who were in-- although they should have been behaving 100%, they didn't behave so well. Sometimes they-- we had scenes sometimes, and people-- the nerves were on edge. And people were sometimes shouting. And people were not always behaving as well as they should have behaved in a condition of complete hide. But we must have been extra-- something, somehow, there was a luck that was difficult to-- to explain. So there were about-- why Koper started it, as I said, there was probably material reasons for that to start. But later, it turned into something different. And then-- so we were altogether about 10 or 11 people there. There was my Aunt Hania, with her friend, a woman called Berta. And then there was my father, and I, and a whole family of a judge, Miller, of his daughter, and his mother, and his sister, I think. But the mother, the wife-- he was himself, I think he-- he was-- I'm not sure what happened to him. But his wife was outside, was not at Koper's. And her family-- and her family of-- and the German Jew, Rolf Neuding, who was-- who didn't even speak Polish, because he was really German. And he spoke only German. And his girlfriend, and her sister, and the sister's son, so there were about, altogether 10, or 11, or 12 people in those two rooms. And there was a lodger as well, who was called [POLISH], that means one who has luck, although his luck was doubtful. But he was a young chap, who was living from smuggling. And he was-- they were all-- the point that I was trying to make was that they were all very simple people, not at all educated, highly educated, or-- really, pretty-- very, very simple. And they saved us. And nobody ever denounced us. So it is all pretty amazing. Not only that, but there were-- they didn't even-- Marta and Copper, they didn't keep so under completely under cover, because few times a year, there were family feasts. There was a birthday, his birthday, her birthday, anniversary, something like that. And then they would invite even more family. They would invite-- Marta's sister who would come, with her husband who was a drunkard, who was a perpetual drunkard. And his sister-- her sister was a market woman. She was selling in one of the Warsaw markets. And so they were there, and some other family. And everybody would get terribly drunk, terribly drunk on vodka. I was even drunk. Children were-- there was another child there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was not such a close conspiracy. And to add to that, Koper was member of the underground organization, Polish. But the underground organization was a very right wing organization. So they all absolutely hated Jews. So they had-- so they couldn't know anything. So they had meetings in one room. And we were sitting in the second room. And we were listening to the opinions, like it is good that the Jews are now disappeared from Poland, or that Hitler did a good job for us, or something like that, you know? So they were here. So he belonged to this right wing organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they, of course, were meeting there. And we were sitting in the next room. And nobody knew anything. So it was altogether unbelievable--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all absolutely unbelievable that we survived. If the things got really bad, bad, we went into the cellar, which was under the second room. But the cellar, frankly, if you lifted the carpet, you saw the trapdoor to the cellar. So the cellar was only a hiding place in name, but not-- not really-- it was not a proper hiding place.

speaker: interviewee
text: If somebody would really look for us there, they would find us in 10 minutes at the longest, or less, you know? And we survived there for almost-- well, for a year and a half, almost, for-- because I was there at Koper's from mid-May '43, till mid-September '44, when the Russians finally came and conquered Praga, that part of Warsaw, and liberated us. So it was one month-- one year and four months, 16 months, that I didn't leave the flat for one moment. And neither did anybody else, except for-- well, my mother used to come. She used to-- my mother was not in hiding. Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in Warsaw. She was still continuing in her existence of moving from one place to another. But she had it easier without me. So she managed to continue like that. And we stayed with-- at Koper's. But she was coming more or less regularly from every few weeks, every four weeks or five weeks. And one other woman was-- this Mrs. Miller was coming occasionally. So there was some contact with the outside world. And we were well informed. We were well informed, because everything was excellently informed because Warsaw was absolutely full of underground organizations. There were-- there was a press. We go the underground press. Copper was getting underground newspapers. He was getting, continuously, underground newspapers, which were giving you all the information concerning the progress of the war, and all was happening in the fronts, and so on. Moreover, we were having Polish language newspapers, German language newspapers. Earth the German Jew, was sitting always in the kitchen, and was reading the communique in the German newspapers, and was-- had a map, and was moving little pins on the map, to see where the Germans were and where the Soviets were. And from summer-- and it was getting more and more clear that there is-- really, that this time, the Russians are coming nearer. They were coming nearer. There were great battles in '43. And after that, in other Kursk area, still deep, deep in Russia. But then they started coming nearer, and nearer, and nearer.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of the cassette number ten.
